response:
# Async Tab Panel Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users, keyboard-only users, low vision users, motor-impaired users, mobile users > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, keyboard-only navigation **Feature:** A reusable Vue.js tab interface where selecting a tab asynchronously loads its associated panel content, with loading, error, retry, and optional disabled states. **Risk Level:** Medium **Component/Page Type:** Tab Panel with Dynamic Content --- ## Scope & Context This plan covers a reusable async tab component in a Vue.js web application. The component contains a tab list, multiple selectable tabs, one visible tab panel at a time, async content loading that may take 1-3 seconds, optional disabled tabs, and error recovery through retry actions. The primary accessibility risk is that the APG Tabs pattern assumes tab-panel relationships are clear and state changes are programmatically available. Async loading adds extra risk: screen reader users must know when content is loading, when it has finished, and when loading failed. Keyboard users also need predictable focus behavior when moving between tabs and when retrying failed content. This plan uses the WAI-ARIA APG Tabs pattern: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/tabs/ The plan assumes **manual tab activation**, not automatic activation, because panel content loads asynchronously. This is important. In the APG pattern, automatic activation is recommended only when panels display without noticeable latency. Here, arrowing through tabs could trigger repeated network requests and unexpected announcements, so selection should happen with `Enter` or `Space`. What this plan is not claiming: it does not guarantee API success, caching behavior, or visual design quality. It only specifies the accessibility contract for the tab interaction, async state communication, and error recovery. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan The component should use semantic sectioning around the tab component if it appears as a meaningful page section. The tab interface itself follows the APG Tabs structure. ### Structure Diagram ```html

[Section title]

[Loaded panel content]

Loading Details…

[Unavailable content or no rendered content]
[Async tab status messages]
``` ### Semantic Requirements The outer wrapper should be a `section` only when the tab component represents a meaningful region of the page. It must be labelled by a visible heading. This supports WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships and WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels. The tab container must use `role="tablist"` and an accessible name via `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`. This satisfies WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value. Each tab should be a native `button` with `role="tab"`. Native buttons preserve keyboard activation behavior and avoid recreating button semantics manually. This supports WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard and WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value. Each panel must use `role="tabpanel"` and must be associated with its tab using `aria-labelledby`. Each tab must reference its panel using `aria-controls`. These relationships satisfy WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships and WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value. Only the active panel should be visible. Inactive panels should use `hidden` or conditional rendering. Do not leave inactive panels visually hidden but keyboard focusable. This supports WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order and WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence. --- ## Interaction Pattern Design ### Interactive Elements Table | Widget | APG Pattern | Keyboard Model | ARIA Attributes | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Tab list | APG Tabs: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/tabs/ | `Tab` moves focus into active tab; arrows move between tabs | `role="tablist"`, `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Tab | APG Tabs | `Left/Right` moves focus; `Home/End` moves focus; `Enter/Space` selects focused tab | `role="tab"`, `aria-selected`, `aria-controls`, `tabindex`, optional `aria-disabled` | 2.1.1, 2.4.3, 4.1.2 | | Tab panel | APG Tabs | `Tab` from selected tab moves into panel or next focusable element | `role="tabpanel"`, `aria-labelledby`, `aria-busy`, `tabindex="0"` when needed | 1.3.1, 2.4.3, 4.1.2, 4.1.3 | | Retry button | APG Button: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/ | `Enter/Space` retries loading | Native `button`, optional `aria-describedby` | 2.1.1, 3.3.1, 4.1.2 | ### Keyboard Model The component should use **manual activation**: - `Tab`: Moves focus into the tab list, landing on the currently selected tab. - `Shift+Tab`: Moves focus to the previous focusable element before the tab list. - `Right Arrow`: Moves focus to the next enabled tab without selecting it. - `Left Arrow`: Moves focus to the previous enabled tab without selecting it. - `Home`: Moves focus to the first enabled tab. - `End`: Moves focus to the last enabled tab. - `Enter`: Selects the focused tab and starts loading its panel if content is not already loaded. - `Space`: Selects the focused tab and starts loading its panel if content is not already loaded. - `Tab` from selected tab: Moves focus to the active tab panel if the panel has `tabindex="0"`, or to the first focusable element inside the panel if one exists. - Disabled tabs: Must not activate. Arrow navigation may either skip disabled tabs or focus them while preventing activation. This plan recommends **skipping disabled tabs** to reduce dead stops for keyboard users. Manual activation is required because async loading creates latency. Automatic activation on arrow movement would cause unexpected loading, repeated announcements, and possible API churn. This is grounded in the APG Tabs pattern guidance and WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard, WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order, and WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages. ### Screen Reader Announcements On tab focus, the screen reader should announce: - Tab name - Role: tab - Selected state - Position where supported, such as “1 of 4” - Disabled state where applicable Example expected announcement: “Details, tab, not selected, 2 of 4.” On selection: “Details, selected.” During loading: “Loading Details.” On completion: “Details loaded.” On error: “Details failed to load. Retry available.” --- ## ARIA Attribute Plan ### Tablist - `role="tablist"`: Identifies the composite tab widget. WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-label="[Tab group label]"` or `aria-labelledby="[heading-id]"`: Provides an accessible name for the tab list. WCAG 2.4.6 and 4.1.2. - Optional `aria-orientation="horizontal"` only if needed. Horizontal is the implicit default for tablist. Use `aria-orientation="vertical"` if vertical layout is implemented. WCAG 4.1.2. ### Tabs - `role="tab"`: Identifies each tab. WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-selected="true"` on the active tab and `"false"` on inactive tabs. WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-controls="[tabpanel-id]"`: Links each tab to its controlled panel. WCAG 1.3.1 and 4.1.2. - `tabindex="0"` on the active/focusable tab and `tabindex="-1"` on the others, using roving tabindex. WCAG 2.4.3 and 4.1.2. - `aria-disabled="true"` for unavailable tabs that remain visible in the tab list. WCAG 4.1.2. - Do not use `disabled` on tab buttons if the disabled tab must remain discoverable to assistive technology. Native `disabled` removes the button from the tab sequence and can reduce discoverability. ### Tab Panels - `role="tabpanel"`: Identifies the content panel. WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-labelledby="[tab-id]"`: Names the panel by its controlling tab. WCAG 1.3.1 and 4.1.2. - `aria-busy="true"` while async content is loading and `"false"` when complete or failed. WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages. - `tabindex="0"` when the panel does not contain a naturally focusable element near the start. This allows keyboard users to move from the tab into panel content. WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order. - `hidden` on inactive panels, or conditional rendering of inactive panels. WCAG 1.3.2 and 2.4.3. ### Live Region Use one shared live region for tab-loading status messages: - `aria-live="polite"`: Announces non-urgent async updates without interrupting the user. WCAG 4.1.3. - `aria-atomic="true"`: Ensures the whole status message is announced. WCAG 4.1.3. - Do not create one live region per tab or inside repeated tab templates. That risks duplicate or noisy announcements. Use `role="alert"` only for critical failures that require immediate attention. For ordinary tab load failures, `aria-live="polite"` is sufficient. --- ## Focus Management Plan Focus should stay on the selected tab after activation. It should not automatically move into the panel when a tab is selected. Rationale: In the APG Tabs pattern, the tab list is a composite widget. Keeping focus on the selected tab preserves orientation and allows users to continue moving between tabs with arrow keys. Automatically moving focus into async panel content would interrupt exploration and make it harder to compare tabs. This supports WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order and APG Tabs behavior. ### Focus Order 1. Previous page content. 2. Tab list, entering on the currently selected tab. 3. Active tab panel or first focusable element inside the active panel. 4. Interactive controls inside the panel, such as links, form fields, or retry button. 5. Next page content. ### Roving Tabindex Only one tab should be in the page tab order at a time: - Selected/focused tab: `tabindex="0"` - Other enabled tabs: `tabindex="-1"` - Disabled tabs: `tabindex="-1"` and `aria-disabled="true"` Arrow keys move focus among enabled tabs by updating which tab has `tabindex="0"`. ### Async Focus Behavior When a tab is selected: 1. Focus remains on the selected tab. 2. The active panel becomes visible. 3. The panel receives `aria-busy="true"`. 4. A polite live region announces loading. 5. When content loads, focus remains on the selected tab. 6. The user can press `Tab` to enter the loaded panel content. When loading fails: 1. Focus remains on the selected tab unless the user has already moved focus elsewhere. 2. The panel displays an error message and a retry button. 3. The live region announces the failure. 4. The retry button is reachable by pressing `Tab` from the selected tab. 5. Activating retry does not move focus unless the retry button is removed after success; if removed, focus should move to the panel container or first meaningful focusable element in the loaded content. What this plan is not claiming: focus should never move into a panel. If future product requirements make tab selection equivalent to navigation into a content area, that decision must be re-planned and tested with screen readers. --- ## State Communication Design ### State Communication Table | State | Visual Indicator | Programmatic Indicator | ARIA Attribute | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Tab selected | Active underline, text weight, selected styling | Selected state exposed on tab | `aria-selected="true"` | 4.1.2 | | Tab not selected | Default tab styling | Not selected state exposed | `aria-selected="false"` | 4.1.2 | | Focused tab | Visible focus ring | DOM focus on tab | `tabindex="0"` with focus | 2.4.7, 2.4.13, 4.1.2 | | Disabled tab | Muted styling plus visible “Unavailable” or disabled treatment | Disabled state exposed | `aria-disabled="true"` | 1.4.1, 4.1.2 | | Panel loading | Spinner plus “Loading…” text | Busy state and live announcement | `aria-busy="true"`, `aria-live="polite"` | 4.1.3 | | Panel loaded | Content replaces loading message | Busy state cleared; completion announced | `aria-busy="false"` plus live message | 4.1.3 | | Load failed | Error icon, error text, retry button | Error text in panel, live announcement | `aria-live="polite"` or `role="alert"` if critical | 3.3.1, 4.1.3 | | Retry available | Visible retry button | Native button role/name | Native `button`; optional `aria-describedby` | 2.1.1, 4.1.2 | | Inactive panel | Hidden from sighted users | Hidden from accessibility tree and focus order | `hidden` or conditional rendering | 1.3.2, 2.4.3 | ### Loading State Each panel should expose loading with both visible and programmatic communication: - Visible: spinner plus text such as “Loading Details…” - Programmatic: active panel has `aria-busy="true"` - Announcement: shared live region says “Loading Details.” - Completion: shared live region says “Details loaded.” - WCAG: 4.1.3 Status Messages, 4.1.2 Name Role Value The spinner must be decorative if the text already communicates loading. Mark it `aria-hidden="true"` or implement it as CSS only. This supports WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content. ### Error State When content fails to load: - Keep the selected tab selected. - Show the tab panel with an error message and retry button. - Error message should be specific: “Details could not be loaded. Check your connection and try again.” - Retry button text should be explicit: “Retry loading Details.” - Use the shared live region to announce: “Details failed to load. Retry available.” - If the retry button needs additional explanation, associate it with the error text using `aria-describedby`. - WCAG: 3.3.1 Error Identification, 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions, 4.1.3 Status Messages Do not link the tab itself to the error message with `aria-describedby` by default. That can cause the error to be repeated every time the user arrows through tabs. Prefer announcing the error once through the live region and placing the error in the active panel. Use `aria-describedby` on the tab only if the tab remains in an error state after the user leaves and returns, and the description is short. ### Disabled Tabs Disabled tabs should remain visible if users need to understand that content exists but is unavailable. - Use `aria-disabled="true"`. - Prevent click, `Enter`, and `Space` activation. - Skip disabled tabs during arrow navigation unless product requirements demand discoverability by arrow focus. - If disabled reason matters, include visible text or an accessible description, such as “Reports, unavailable until data sync completes.” - WCAG: 1.4.1 Use of Color, 4.1.2 Name Role Value --- ## Visual Accessibility Plan Text contrast must meet WCAG 1.4.3: - Normal text: minimum 4.5:1. - Large text: minimum 3:1. - Disabled tab text should still be readable where possible. If reduced contrast is used for disabled state, the disabled state must also be communicated through text, icon, or programmatic state. Non-text contrast must meet WCAG 1.4.11: - Focus ring, selected tab indicator, tab borders, error icons, retry button boundaries, and spinner affordances must meet 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors. Color must not be the only indicator per WCAG 1.4.1: - Selected tab: use color plus underline, border, shape, or text weight. - Error: use color plus error text and icon. - Disabled: use color plus `aria-disabled` and visible unavailable styling. Focus indicators must satisfy WCAG 2.4.7 and 2.4.13: - Use `:focus-visible`. - Use a two-color focus indicator where possible, such as a dark outline plus light offset shadow. - Ensure focused tabs are not obscured by sticky headers or mobile browser UI, per WCAG 2.4.11. Touch targets must satisfy WCAG 2.5.8: - Tabs and retry buttons must be at least 24x24 CSS pixels. - Recommended target size is 44x44 CSS pixels, especially on mobile. - Horizontal tab lists on mobile should support wrapping or horizontal scroll with visible affordance. If horizontally scrollable, keyboard focus must scroll the focused tab into view. Text and layout must support zoom: - Use relative units. - At 200% zoom, tabs and panels must remain usable without loss of content. - At 400% zoom / 320px equivalent width, the component must reflow without requiring two-dimensional scrolling, except where unavoidable for contained tabular content. - WCAG: 1.4.4 Resize Text, 1.4.10 Reflow Motion: - Loading spinners should respect `prefers-reduced-motion`. - For reduced motion, use static loading text or a non-animated indicator. - WCAG: 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions --- ## Content Accessibility Plan Tab labels must be short, descriptive, and unique. Avoid repeated labels like “Info” when multiple tab groups appear on the same page. This supports WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels. The tab list must have an accessible group label, either visible through a heading and `aria-labelledby`, or programmatic through `aria-label`. Visible headings are preferred. Panel content must begin with meaningful content. If the panel contains a complex section, include a heading inside the panel. Avoid duplicating the tab label as the only heading unless it helps orientation. Loading text must include the tab name: “Loading Details…” is better than “Loading…” Completion announcements should include the tab name: “Details loaded.” Error messages should identify the affected tab: “Details could not be loaded.” Retry button text should include the target: “Retry loading Details,” not just “Retry.” If tab labels include icons, decorative icons must be hidden with `aria-hidden="true"`. If an icon communicates state, provide equivalent text or accessible description. WCAG 1.1.1. DOM order must match visual order. Do not reorder tabs visually with CSS in a way that differs from DOM order. WCAG 1.3.2. --- ## Testing Strategy ### Automated Testing Run axe-core against these states: - Initial loaded state. - Focused tab state. - Loading state. - Loaded state after async completion. - Error state with retry button. - Disabled tab state. - Mobile viewport. - 200% zoom-equivalent layout where possible. Axe-core should catch invalid ARIA relationships, missing names, contrast issues, and role misuse. It will not fully verify keyboard behavior, live region announcements, or whether async focus behavior feels coherent. Static analysis should include Vue-compatible accessibility linting where available. The important checks are invalid ARIA attributes, missing button names, and misuse of interactive roles. ### Keyboard Tests Test with real key presses: - `Tab` moves focus to the selected tab. - `Right Arrow` moves focus to the next enabled tab without selecting it. - `Left Arrow` moves focus to the previous enabled tab without selecting it. - `Home` moves focus to the first enabled tab. - `End` moves focus to the last enabled tab. - `Enter` selects the focused tab and starts loading. - `Space` selects the focused tab and starts loading. - `Tab` after selected tab moves to active panel or first focusable item inside it. - Disabled tabs cannot be activated. - Retry button can be reached and activated with `Enter` and `Space`. - Focus is never trapped in the tab list or panel. ### Async Tests - Selecting an unloaded tab sets the tab to selected and the panel to visible. - Active panel receives `aria-busy="true"` during fetch. - Loading text is visible. - Shared live region announces loading. - When fetch succeeds, `aria-busy` changes to `"false"`. - Loaded content replaces loading UI. - Shared live region announces completion. - Focus remains on the selected tab unless the user moved it. ### Error Tests - Failed load shows visible error message. - Error message identifies the affected tab. - Retry button has an accessible name that includes the tab name. - Shared live region announces failure. - Retry starts loading again and updates state. - After retry success, focus does not disappear when the retry button is removed. ### Screen Reader Tests Test with NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver: - Tab list is announced as a tab group. - Each tab announces name, role, selected state, and disabled state where applicable. - Arrow key navigation is understandable. - Manual activation does not unexpectedly load content on arrow movement. - Loading announcement is heard once. - Completion announcement is heard once. - Error announcement is heard once and does not repeat excessively. - Active panel is named by the selected tab. - Inactive panels are not reachable. ### DOM Verification Verify rendered DOM, not just component state: - Exactly one tab has `aria-selected="true"`. - Exactly one tab has `tabindex="0"`. - Each tab’s `aria-controls` points to an existing panel id. - Each panel’s `aria-labelledby` points to an existing tab id. - Only active panel is visible/reachable. - Active loading panel has `aria-busy="true"`. - Completed or failed panel has `aria-busy="false"`. - Disabled tab has `aria-disabled="true"` and does not activate. --- ## Implementation Tasks ### Task 1: Core Tab Structure 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Reusable Vue tab component - Component tests for ARIA relationships and initial render **Structure Stub:** ```html

[Tab group heading]

[Panel content]
``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `role="tablist"`: Identifies tab group. WCAG 4.1.2. - `role="tab"`: Identifies each tab. WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-selected`: Communicates selected state. WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-controls`: Associates tab with panel. WCAG 1.3.1, 4.1.2. - `role="tabpanel"`: Identifies panel. WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-labelledby`: Names panel from tab. WCAG 1.3.1, 4.1.2. - `tabindex`: Implements roving tabindex. WCAG 2.4.3. **Keyboard Interactions:** - `Tab`: enter selected tab. - `ArrowLeft` / `ArrowRight`: move focus among enabled tabs. - `Home` / `End`: move focus to first or last enabled tab. - `Enter` / `Space`: select focused tab. **Tests:** - One selected tab on initial render. - Tab-panel id relationships are valid. - Inactive panels are hidden or not rendered. - Roving tabindex is correct. **WCAG Criteria:** - 1.3.1 Info and Relationships - 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence - 2.1.1 Keyboard - 2.4.3 Focus Order - 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value --- ### Task 2: Manual Activation Keyboard Model 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Vue keyboard handler logic - Keyboard interaction tests **Keyboard Interactions:** - Arrow keys move focus only. - `Enter` and `Space` select the focused tab. - Disabled tabs are skipped or focusable but not activatable; this plan recommends skipped. - `Tab` exits tab list into active panel. **Tests:** - Arrowing does not start async load. - `Enter` starts async load. - `Space` starts async load. - `Home` and `End` work. - Disabled tab cannot be selected. **WCAG Criteria:** - 2.1.1 Keyboard - 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap - 2.4.3 Focus Order - 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value --- ### Task 3: Async Loading State 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Async state handling in tab component - Loading state UI - Live region utility or component **Structure Stub:** ```html
[spinner]

Loading [tab label]…

Loading [tab label].
``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `aria-busy="true"` during loading. WCAG 4.1.3. - `aria-busy="false"` after success or failure. WCAG 4.1.3. - `aria-live="polite"` for async status messages. WCAG 4.1.3. - `aria-atomic="true"` for complete message announcement. WCAG 4.1.3. - `aria-hidden="true"` on decorative spinner. WCAG 1.1.1. **Tests:** - Loading message is visible. - Live region receives loading message once. - `aria-busy` toggles correctly. - Spinner is hidden from assistive tech if decorative. - Focus remains on selected tab. **WCAG Criteria:** - 1.1.1 Non-text Content - 2.4.3 Focus Order - 4.1.3 Status Messages --- ### Task 4: Error and Retry State 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Error panel state - Retry button - Error tests **Structure Stub:** ```html

[Tab label] could not be loaded. Check your connection and try again.

``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `aria-busy="false"` after failure. WCAG 4.1.3. - `aria-describedby` on retry button if extra error context is needed. WCAG 1.3.1. - Shared live region announces failure. WCAG 4.1.3. **Keyboard Interactions:** - `Tab` reaches retry button. - `Enter` or `Space` activates retry. - If retry button is removed after success while focused, focus moves to the panel container or first meaningful focusable item in loaded content. **Tests:** - Error is visible and specific. - Retry button has descriptive accessible name. - Retry restarts loading. - Failure is announced once. - Focus is preserved or intentionally moved after retry success. **WCAG Criteria:** - 1.3.1 Info and Relationships - 2.1.1 Keyboard - 2.4.3 Focus Order - 3.3.1 Error Identification - 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions - 4.1.3 Status Messages --- ### Task 5: Disabled Tabs and Mobile Support 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Disabled tab state handling - Responsive tab styling - Mobile keyboard/touch tests **ARIA Attributes:** - `aria-disabled="true"` on disabled tabs. WCAG 4.1.2. - `tabindex="-1"` for disabled tabs if skipped in arrow navigation. WCAG 2.4.3. **Visual Requirements:** - Disabled state is not color-only. - Touch targets are at least 24x24 CSS pixels, with 44x44 recommended. - Focused tabs scroll into view in horizontally scrollable mobile layouts. **Tests:** - Disabled tabs are announced as unavailable. - Disabled tabs do not activate by click, `Enter`, or `Space`. - Mobile layout preserves all tabs and panel access. - Focus indicator remains visible on mobile and at zoom. **WCAG Criteria:** - 1.4.1 Use of Color - 1.4.10 Reflow - 2.4.7 Focus Visible - 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured - 2.5.8 Target Size - 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value --- ## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoints | Checkpoint | After Task | Focus Areas | |---|---|---| | 1 | Core Tab Structure | APG Tabs mapping, valid tab-panel relationships, semantic structure | | 2 | Keyboard Model | Manual activation, roving tabindex, arrow/Home/End behavior | | 3 | Async Loading | `aria-busy`, live region behavior, focus stability | | 4 | Error and Retry | Error announcement, retry accessibility, focus after retry | | 5 | Disabled/Mobile | Disabled tab behavior, mobile target size, focus visibility, reflow | --- ## Architecture Overview The component uses one APG Tabs composite widget with manual activation, roving tabindex, one active tab panel, one shared polite live region, and explicit async states per panel. The key design decision is that focus remains on the selected tab after activation; users enter the panel with `Tab`. ARIA patterns used: - APG Tabs for tablist, tabs, and tabpanels. - APG Button for retry actions. - Live region pattern for loading, completion, and failure announcements. --- ## Failure Modes This plan prevents these likely failures: - Arrowing through tabs accidentally triggers multiple async loads. - Screen reader users hear no loading or completion announcement. - Inactive panels remain reachable by keyboard. - `aria-selected` and visible selected state drift apart. - Tabs lack valid `aria-controls` relationships. - Panels lack valid `aria-labelledby` relationships. - Error messages appear visually but are never announced. - Retry buttons are named only “Retry” without context. - Disabled tabs look disabled but still activate. - Loading spinner is announced without useful text. - Focus jumps into panels unexpectedly and disrupts tab exploration. --- ## References - WAI-ARIA APG Tabs Pattern: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/tabs/ - WAI-ARIA APG Button Pattern: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/ - WCAG 2.2 Quick Reference: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/quickref/ - WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/
